---
title: "PS8: Strings and Date-Times"
subtitle: "STAT 133"
author: "Yuan Xie"
format: 
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    theme: simplex
embed-resources: true
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, error = TRUE)
library(tidyverse)
```

::: {.callout-note icon=false}

## General Instructions

-   Download the template file `ps8.qmd` to answer the questions.

-   Rename `ps8.qmd` to `ps8-first-last.qmd`, where `first` and `last`
    are your first and last names (e.g. `ps8-gaston-sanchez.qmd`).

-   Render your qmd file to an HTML file and submit both your qmd and HTML files to the corresponding assignment submission in bCourses.

:::

# Strings

## _Wicked_ String Matches

Below is a dataframe named `users` that contains the user's name, email address, and country of origin.

```{r}
#| echo: false
# This cell creates the dataframe for this part of the 
# assignment. Do not edit the contents of this
# cell.
names <- c(
  "Glinda Upland",
  NA,
  "Dorothy Gale",
  "Oscar Diggs",
  "Lion",
  "Emily Gale",
  "Elphaba Thropp",
  "Boq Woodman",
  "Henry Gale",
  "Nessarose Thropp",
  "Fiyero Tigelaar"
)

emails <- c(
  "popular.witch@emeraldcity.gov",
  "munchkin1@yellowbrick.org",
  "dorothy_gale@ozmail.com",
  "wizard101@emeraldcity.gov",
  "very_brave_lion67@ozmail.com",
  "emily_gale@ozmail.com",
  "wwwest@ozmail.com",
  "tin.man@yellowbrick.org",
  NA,
  "wweast@ozmail.com",
  "brainiac@emeraldcity.gov"
)

country <- c(
  "Gillikin (GLK)",
  "Munchkinland (MKL)",
  "United States (USA)",
  NA,
  NA,
  "United States (USA)",
  "Munchkinland (MKL)",
  "Munchkinland (MKL)",
  "United States (USA)",
  "Munchkinland (MKL)",
  "Vinkus (VKS)"
)

users <- data.frame(
  name = names,
  email = emails,
  country = country,
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

users
```

Answer the following questions using `stringr` and regex patterns.

a) Get the number of users whose name begins with the letter `"E"`. You may want to use `sum()` for this.

```{r}
sum(str_detect(users$name, "^E"), na.rm = TRUE)
```

\

b) Get the number of users whose emails do not contain a number. You will need to account for the presence of `NA`.

```{r}
sum(!str_detect(users$email, "[0-9]"), na.rm = TRUE)
```

\

c) Extract the users' emails that contain at least one underscore: `"_"`.

```{r}
users$email[!is.na(users$email) & str_detect(users$email, "_")]
```

\

d) Use lookaheads/behinds to extract the country abbreviation (e.g. `VKS`) from the `country` column.

```{r}
str_extract(users$country, "(?<=\\()[A-Z]+(?=\\))")
```

\

e) Use lookaheads/behinds to extract the users' last names from the `name` column.

```{r}
str_extract(users$name, "(?<= )[A-Za-z]+$")
```

\

f) Use lookaheads/behinds to exctract the "local part" of users' email addresses: e.g. `"munchkin1"` from `"munchkin1@yellowbrick.org"`

```{r}
str_extract(users$email, ".+(?=@)")
```

\

g) Use lookaheads/behinds to extract the domain of users' email addresses: e.g. `"yellowbrick.org"` from `"munchkin1@yellowbrick.org"`

```{r}
str_extract(users$email, "(?<=@).+")
```

\

h) Use lookaheads/behinds and to get the number of users with a ".gov" email address.

```{r}
sum(str_detect(users$email, "\\.gov$"), na.rm = TRUE)
```

\

# Dates and Times

## You in Date-Time

We will be using your birthdate to practice storing date-time objects in different forms. If you are not sure of the exact hour-minute-second of your birth (likely), you are welcome to use your best guess. If you are uncomfortable using your actual birthdate, you may use the date July 31st, 1980, 5:31:07 AM UTC (Harry Potter's birthday) instead.

### Different Formats

a) Use `as.POSIXct()` to represent your birthdate as a `POSIXct` vector of the form `"YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS"`. Don't forget to specify the timezone!

```{r}
as.POSIXct("2005-03-25 09:33:00", tz = "Asia/Shanghai")
```

\

b) Now use `as.POSIXct()` to input your birthday using `format = "%b %d, %Y %I:%M %p"`.

```{r}
as.POSIXct("Mar 25, 2005 9:33 am", format = "%b %d, %Y %I:%M %p", tz = "Asia/Shanghai")
```

\

c) Store your birthday as a `POSIXct` vector, and then use `unclass` to find the Unix timestamp associated with your birthday.

```{r}
birthday <- as.POSIXct("Mar 25, 2005 9:33 am", format = "%b %d, %Y %I:%M %p", tz = "Asia/Shanghai")
unclass(birthday)
```

\

d) Use `lubridate` and a string that expresses your birthday in a format similar to `"DD-MM-YYYY HH:MM"`.

```{r}
dmy_hm("25-03-2005 09:33", tz = "Asia/Shanghai")
```

\

e) Use `lubridate` and a string that expresses your birthday in a format similar to `"Oct 10, 2025 1:00 pm"`.

```{r}
mdy_hm("03-25-2005 9:30 am", tz = "Asia/Shanghai")
```

\

### Extracting Information

Now recall if we have a date-time object, we can extract components using `year()`, `month()`, `day()`, etc. Answer the following questions using `lubridate` and R commands.

a) What day of the week were you born on? Express your answer as the name of the day: e.g. Thursday.

```{r}
wday(birthday, label = TRUE, abbr = FALSE)
```

\

b) The first day of classes this semester fell on which week of the current year?

```{r}
week(ymd("2026-01-20"))
```

\

c) In which year did the Unix timestamp `-1103932800` occur? Recall that negative Unix timestamps mean the date was before $1970$.

```{r}
year(as.POSIXct(-1103932800, origin = "1970-01-01", tz = "UTC"))
```

\

## The End of an Epoch

Recall that Unix Time is represented by the number of seconds since `"1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC"`. This represents the start of the Unix Epoch. However, many older systems store Unix integers as 32 bit integers. i.e. They use 32 zeros/ones to represent the number. You don't have to fully understand this, but that means that the largest Unix timestamp in this form is $2^{31} - 1 = 2,147,483,647$.

a) Use `as.POSIXct()` to store the unix timestamp of `0` (The beginning of the Unix Epoch) as a `POSIXct` vector named `epoch`.

```{r}
epoch <- as.POSIXct(0, origin = "1970-01-01", tz = "UTC")
epoch
```

\

b) Recall that these `POSIXct` vectors respect arithmetic using Unix timestamps. Add the largest possible Unix timestamp to `epoch`. The result will be the largest date-time that can be represented on systems using 32-bit Unix timestamps.

```{r}
epoch + (2^31 - 1)
```

\

This is known as the "Year 2038 Problem" and it has it's own Wikipedia page: [Year 2038 Problem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Year_2038_problem). The use of 32-bit integers creates a similar problem in older video games, such as _Old School Runescape_, where the maximum number of a singular item a player can hold is $2,147,483,647$.
